models.themeCategory: A top-level grouping for themes: virtue, emotion, doctrine, relationship, condition, action, attribute of God.
models.theme: A conceptual thread that traces across the canon with modern framing, scholarly definition, and hierarchical children.
models.themePassage: A passage where a theme surfaces, with contextual annotation and source tier.
models.connectedCharacter: A character who embodies this theme, with a brief annotation.
models.traceSegment: One of 66 book segments in the density trace bar, with an occurrence count.
models.threadPrompt: The rotating discover-equivalent that traces a theme across the canon in bridge voice.

relationships: Theme may have child Themes (parent_theme_id hierarchy)
Theme has many ThemePassages grouped by era
Theme has many related Themes (horizontal connections)

threadPrompt.name: Exile
threadPrompt.hook: Trace the thread of exile — it starts with Adam leaving the garden, runs through Hagar in the desert, peaks in Babylon, and echoes in every person who's ever felt displaced from home.

themes.name: Forgiveness
themes.modernFraming: The decision to stop charging rent for the space someone occupies in your head

themes.name: Faith
themes.modernFraming: Trusting the rope before you can see what it's attached to

themes.name: Love
themes.modernFraming: Choosing someone's good when it costs you something

themes.name: Grief
themes.modernFraming: The weight you carry when the world subtracts someone from it

themes.name: Joy
themes.modernFraming: Not happiness — the quieter thing underneath it that stays when circumstances don't

themes.name: Fear
themes.modernFraming: The alarm system that sometimes fires when there's a fire and sometimes when there's toast

themes.name: Covenant
themes.modernFraming: A promise so binding it reshapes both parties — not a contract you can exit, but an identity you enter

themes.name: Redemption
themes.modernFraming: Buying something back that was always yours — the receipt just got lost

themes.name: Exile
themes.modernFraming: The ache of being somewhere that isn't home and not being sure home still exists

themes.name: Suffering
themes.modernFraming: The question that has no answer and the silence that follows it

themes.name: Identity
themes.modernFraming: The answer to 'who are you?' when no one is watching

themes.name: Obedience
themes.modernFraming: Doing the thing before understanding why — and finding the understanding comes after

themes.name: Grace
themes.modernFraming: Getting what you didn't earn and not getting what you did — at the same time

themes.name: Justice
themes.modernFraming: The universe insisting that things be made right, even when 'right' is expensive

selectedProfile.name: Exile
selectedProfile.modernFraming: The ache of being somewhere that isn't home and not being sure home still exists. Exile isn't just a historical event — it's a recurring human condition. Every person who's moved to a city where they know no one, every immigrant who dreams in one language and lives in another, every kid sitting alone at lunch for the third week in a row. The Bible returns to this theme because it knows displacement is universal.
selectedProfile.scholarlyDefinition: The forced removal of a people from their homeland, particularly the Babylonian exile of 586 BCE and the earlier Assyrian deportation of the northern kingdom in 722 BCE. Theologically, exile represents the consequences of covenant unfaithfulness and the tension between God's judgment and God's promise of restoration.

patriarchal.annotation: The first exile. Not a geographic relocation but an existential one — humanity losing access to the presence of God. Every exile after this is an echo.
Sent away with a child and a water skin. Exile imposed by someone else's promise — she didn't break a covenant, she was collateral damage in one.

monarchy.annotation: The exile that defined a people. Not just displacement — the destruction of the temple, the seat of God's presence. When home and God and identity are all in the same place and all three are taken at once.

exile.annotation: Exile as assimilation pressure. New name, new diet, new language — but Daniel draws a line. The question isn't 'where are you?' but 'who are you when everything familiar has been replaced?'
The exile's anthem. 'How can we sing the Lord's song in a foreign land?' The raw grief of displacement turned into poetry — which is itself an act of resistance against forgetting.

exile.passageTitle: Seek the welfare of the city
exile.annotation: The most counterintuitive instruction in Scripture. Bloom where you're displaced. Build houses in enemy territory. Pray for the city that conquered you. Exile as mission field.

life-of-christ.annotation: Self-imposed exile. He didn't lose his home — he left it. The far country isn't a place, it's a decision. And the return is possible because the father never moved.

early-church.annotation: Exile reframed as identity. Not a punishment but a calling — to live as resident aliens whose citizenship is elsewhere. The early church turned displacement into a theological position.

connectedCharacters.name: Hagar
connectedCharacters.annotation: Twice exiled — first from Egypt to Canaan as a servant, then from Abraham's household into the desert. Seen by God both times.

connectedCharacters.name: Daniel
connectedCharacters.annotation: Taken to Babylon as a teenager, given a new name, pressured to assimilate. Drew boundaries that defined his identity in exile for decades.

connectedCharacters.name: Ruth
connectedCharacters.annotation: Chose exile voluntarily — left Moab for a foreign land because loyalty mattered more than belonging.

connectedCharacters.name: Samaritan woman
connectedCharacters.annotation: Social exile within her own community. She didn't leave home — home left her. The well at noon is exile measured in hours, not miles.
